summary: Microsoft's Run and RunOnce registry documentation confirms that the Run key is a supported autostart surface when a user logs on. The current app writes ssh-agent.exe into HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, so this record now has a documented Windows autostart contract.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: lines 39-46: Use `Run` registry keys to make a program run when a user logs on. The `Run` key makes the program run every time the user logs on.
validation_proof.notes: The Windows Run key documentation establishes the supported autostart surface used by the current app implementation.

setting.name: SSH Agent Auto-start
setting.casual_explanation: ssh-agent keeps SSH keys loaded so Git and terminal sessions can use them more easily. Auto-starting it can make developer login sessions more convenient.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it so SSH-based Git and remote workflows work immediately after sign-in without manually starting the agent.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Automatically starting ssh-agent can make development workflows smoother, but it also starts an extra background process for the current user.
setting.professional_notes: OpenSSH documents ssh-agent as the process that stores private keys for public-key authentication. Windows documents the HKCU Run key as a supported way to run a program at logon, and the current app writes ssh-agent.exe there.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: No ssh-agent autostart entry is present for the current user.

allowed_values.label: SSH Agent autostart enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The current user launches ssh-agent at logon by writing the agent executable into the Windows Run key.

targets.notes: The Windows Run key is a supported autostart surface. This record documents the current app's ssh-agent entry as a valid user-logon startup mapping.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes ssh-agent.exe into the current-user Run key, and the Windows Run key documentation confirms that this is a supported logon autostart surface.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed default

states.rationale: Leave the Run key value absent if you do not want ssh-agent to start automatically at logon.

windows_defaults.notes: Absence of the Run key entry means ssh-agent does not auto-start for the current user.

recommended_profiles.label: Auto-start SSH Agent
intended_for: Developers who use SSH-based Git workflows
Users who want ssh-agent available immediately after sign-in
avoid_for: Users who do not want additional background processes at sign-in

states.rationale: The Run key is a supported autostart surface and this command starts ssh-agent at logon.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want ssh-agent available automatically for developer workflows.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to keep sign-in startup minimal.
tradeoffs: ssh-agent starts automatically for the current user.
You can reverse this by removing the Run value.

evidence.title: Run and RunOnce Registry Keys
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the Run key as a supported way to make a program run when a user logs on, for both user and machine scopes.

evidence.title: OpenSSH manual: ssh-agent
evidence.summary: OpenSSH documents ssh-agent as the agent process that stores private keys for public-key authentication.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SSH Agent = C:\Windows\System32\OpenSSH\ssh-agent.exe under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the Run key as a supported logon autostart surface, and the current app writes ssh-agent.exe into that surface for the current user. That is enough to validate the mapping as a documented Windows startup control.
